section.title: The God of All Comfort
section.sectionRationale: The most personal of Paul's letters opens not with rebuke or argument but with a doxology born of pain. Before defending his ministry, Paul blesses the God who had met him in crushing affliction in Asia, where he despaired even of life. He unfolds a theology of comfort: God comforts the afflicted so that they in turn can comfort others, and He teaches His servants to stop trusting themselves and to trust the God who raises the dead.
section.thesis: The God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ is the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, who meets His people in their deepest affliction, teaches them to rely not on themselves but on the God who raises the dead, and comforts them so that the comfort overflows to others.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the greeting to the church and the saints of Achaia; the blessing of the God of all comfort who comforts us so we can comfort others; and the deadly peril in Asia that taught Paul to trust the God who raises the dead, sustained by the prayers of the saints.

units.label: Greeting to the church
units.note: grace and peace from God our Father

verses.text: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, and Timothy our brother, To the church of God in Corinth, together with all the saints throughout Achaia:
Grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

units.label: The God of all comfort
units.note: comforted so that we can comfort others

verses.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of compassion and the God of all {{paraklesis|comfort}},
who comforts us in all our {{thlipsis|troubles}}, so that we can comfort those in any trouble with the comfort we ourselves have received from God.
For just as the sufferings of Christ overflow to us, so also through Christ our comfort overflows.
If we are afflicted, it is for your comfort and salvation; if we are comforted, it is for your comfort, which accomplishes in you patient endurance of the same sufferings we experience.
And our hope for you is sure, because we know that just as you share in our sufferings, so also you will share in our comfort.

units.label: Deliverance from deadly peril
units.note: we despaired of life, that we might trust the God who raises the dead

verses.text: We do not want you to be unaware, brothers, of the hardships we encountered in the province of Asia. We were under a burden far beyond our ability to endure, so that we despaired even of life.
Indeed, we felt we were under the sentence of death, in order that we would not trust in ourselves, but in God, who {{egeiro|raises the dead}}.
He has delivered us from such a deadly peril, and He will deliver us. In Him we have placed our hope that He will yet again deliver us,
as you help us by your prayers. Then many will give thanks on our behalf for the favor shown us in answer to their prayers.

paraklesis.gloss: Comfort, consolation, encouragement, the strengthening that comes from one called alongside to help. The related verb parakaleo sounds through this passage like a refrain, appearing ten times in five verses. It is not a shallow soothing of feelings but the sturdy encouragement God supplies in the midst of trouble, the same word family used of the Holy Spirit, the Paraclete. Paul roots all such comfort in the character of God Himself, the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort.

thlipsis.gloss: Pressure, affliction, tribulation, the crushing weight of hardship. The image is of being pressed or squeezed, as grapes under a press. Paul does not promise believers a life free of thlipsis; he promises the presence of God within it and comfort sufficient to bear it. The same afflictions that press the servant of Christ become the very occasion of God's consolation, which then overflows to others in their own troubles.

egeiro.gloss: To raise up, to awaken, to raise from the dead. It is the standard New Testament word for the resurrection of Jesus and of believers. When Paul says God raises the dead, he reaches for the deepest ground of hope available: the God who conquered the grave can be trusted in any peril, even when death itself seems certain. The lesson of Asia was to stop relying on self and to rely wholly on the God whose power is displayed supremely in resurrection.

basic.context: Paul opens his most personal letter with worship instead of argument. He had passed through an affliction in Asia so severe that he gave up hope of surviving. Out of that darkness he blesses God as the Father of all comfort, the One who comforts His people in their troubles so that they in turn can comfort others. The whole letter, a defense of true gospel ministry, begins from this well of consolation.
basic.hermeneutics: These verses are the doorway to the letter, setting its tone. Read the repeated word comfort as the theme: God comforts the afflicted, and that comfort is meant to flow onward to others. The account of the peril in Asia is not self pity but testimony, showing that even an apostle had to learn to stop trusting himself and to trust the God who raises the dead.
basic.originalLanguages: The keynote is paraklesis, comfort, with its verb sounding ten times in a few verses, the strengthening that comes from one called alongside. God comforts us in every thlipsis, every crushing affliction. And the lesson of Asia was to trust the God who raises the dead, egeiro, the resurrection word, so that hope rests on His power and not on our own.
basic.typology: The pattern of suffering that gives way to comfort, and of a death sentence answered by the God who raises the dead, mirrors the death and resurrection of Christ. As the sufferings of Christ overflow to His servants, so through Christ His comfort overflows, and the servant walks the road of the cross before the crown.
basic.christ: All comfort comes through Christ. God is named as the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, and it is the sufferings of Christ that overflow to Paul, and through Christ that comfort overflows in return. The God who raises the dead is the God who raised Jesus, so every consolation here is grounded in the resurrection of the Son.
basic.god: God is the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, who does not stand aloof from His people's pain but meets them in it. He is sovereign even over deadly peril, delivering, and will deliver, and will yet again deliver. He is the God who raises the dead, and He is glorified as many give thanks for answered prayer.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 40 opens with God's command to comfort His people. Romans 8 promises that present sufferings are not worth comparing with the coming glory. Philippians 1 shows Paul again facing death yet full of hope. 1 Corinthians 15 grounds all hope in the resurrection.
basic.oneStory: The Bible is the story of a God who comes near to a suffering world. Here that God is named the Father of all comfort, who meets His servant in the valley of the shadow of death and teaches him to trust the God who raises the dead. The comfort received becomes comfort given, and the whole church is knit together in shared suffering, shared prayer, and shared consolation in Christ.

context.literary: The letter opens with a standard greeting, then departs from Paul's usual pattern. Where his other letters often move into thanksgiving for the readers, here he breaks into a berakah, a blessing of God, in the Jewish style. The dense repetition of the comfort word group in verses 3 to 7 sets the emotional and theological key for everything that follows, a letter written from and about affliction and consolation.
context.historical: Achaia was the Roman province of southern Greece with Corinth as its capital. The affliction in Asia, the province around Ephesus, is not specified; it may refer to the riot at Ephesus, a severe illness, imprisonment, or some other deadly crisis. Whatever it was, it pressed Paul to the edge of death and reshaped his reliance on God. The Corinthians' prayers had a real part in his deliverance.
context.canonical: This opening establishes 2 Corinthians as the great New Testament treatment of suffering in ministry, where strength is made perfect in weakness. It joins the biblical stream of comfort that runs from Isaiah's Comfort, comfort my people to the promise that God will wipe away every tear in the new creation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To lift the readers' eyes to the character of God before addressing any conflict, teaching them that affliction is the arena of divine comfort, that such comfort is given to be shared, and that the deepest crises are meant to move us from self reliance to trust in the God who raises the dead.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's account of the peril in Asia describes his particular experience, yet the truths drawn from it are prescriptive for all believers: God comforts the afflicted, comfort is to be passed on, self reliance must give way to trust in God, and prayer for one another is a real means God uses.

debates.question: What was the affliction in Asia that brought Paul to despair of life?

views.label: A specific external crisis such as the Ephesian riot or an imprisonment
views.case: Acts records a violent riot at Ephesus, and Paul elsewhere speaks of fighting wild beasts at Ephesus and of many imprisonments; a public, life threatening ordeal fits the language of a deadly peril and a sentence of death.

views.label: A severe illness or unnamed personal ordeal
views.case: Paul gives no details, and the language of a burden far beyond his ability to endure could describe a crushing sickness or an accumulation of trials; the deliberate vagueness may be pastoral, so that any afflicted reader can find themselves in it.

debates.bearing: The precise event cannot be settled and is not the point; Paul withholds the details so that the lesson stands for every reader, that God comforts in affliction and is to be trusted as the One who raises the dead.

debates.question: In what sense do the sufferings of Christ overflow to believers?

views.label: Sufferings endured for Christ's sake and in union with Him
views.case: Paul suffers as Christ's servant and in fellowship with his Lord, filling up in his own flesh what he calls the afflictions of Christ; the sufferings are Christ's because they are borne in His service and by His body.

views.label: A share in the pattern and power of Christ's own passion
views.case: The believer's suffering follows the shape of the cross and leads to the comfort of resurrection life, so that the same movement from death to life seen in Christ is reproduced in His people.

debates.bearing: Both guard against any thought that believers add to the atonement; Christ's saving death is complete and unrepeatable, while His people are joined to Him in a suffering that is real and comforted by His risen life.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is saturated with paraklesis, comfort, the strengthening help of one called alongside, its verb ringing ten times in five verses so that comfort becomes almost the pulse of the paragraph. This comfort is given in the midst of thlipsis, the pressing, crushing affliction that Paul does not escape but is carried through. At the darkest point Paul reaches for egeiro, the resurrection word, confessing God as the One who raises the dead, so that the whole theology of comfort rests on the same power that raised Jesus and will raise us. Comfort, affliction, and resurrection thus bind the paragraph into one hope.

typology.type: Suffering that gives way to comfort
typology.fulfillment: Follows the pattern of Christ's death and resurrection; as His sufferings overflow to His servants, so through Him His comfort overflows, and the road of the cross leads to consolation.

typology.type: The sentence of death answered by the God who raises the dead
typology.fulfillment: Paul's deliverance from deadly peril is a foretaste of the final resurrection, grounded in the God who raised Jesus and will raise all who are His.

study.christ: Christ stands at the center of this comfort. God is blessed as the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, and it is the sufferings of Christ that overflow to Paul, while through Christ the comfort overflows in return. To suffer as a servant of the gospel is to share in the sufferings of the Lord, and to be comforted is to share in His risen life. The God who raises the dead is the God who raised Jesus, so that every consolation offered here flows from the cross and the empty tomb.
study.god: God is revealed as the Father of compassion and the God of all comfort, tender toward His afflicted people and present in their pain rather than distant from it. He is sovereign over life and death, delivering from deadly peril and pledged to deliver again. He is the God who raises the dead, the surest ground of hope, and He is glorified when many give thanks for His mercy. He weaves the prayers of His people into His work of deliverance, honoring their intercession.

crossReferences.note: Comfort, comfort my people, says your God; the fountainhead of the biblical theme of divine comfort.
We suffer with Christ that we may also be glorified with Him; present sufferings are outweighed by coming glory.
Afflicted but not crushed; always carrying the death of Jesus so His life may also be revealed in us.
Paul again faces possible death, trusting deliverance through the prayers of the saints and the Spirit of Christ.

study.oneStory: Scripture tells of a God who does not abandon His people to their afflictions but comes to comfort them. Here He is named the Father of all comfort, who met His servant at the point of despair and taught him to trust the God who raises the dead. The comfort received becomes comfort given, sufferings and prayers are shared across the whole church, and the hope of resurrection carries God's people through every trouble until He wipes away every tear.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe God at the opening of the letter?

options.text: The Father of compassion and the God of all comfort.
options.note: Verse 3 names God precisely this way, setting the theme of the passage.

options.text: The God who keeps His people from all suffering.
options.note: God comforts in affliction; He does not promise a life free of it.

options.text: A distant judge who watches from afar.
options.note: Paul stresses God's nearness and compassion, not distance.

options.text: A God concerned only with Israel.
options.note: This comfort is for the whole church, Jew and Gentile alike in Christ.

quiz.q: Why does God comfort us in our troubles, according to verse 4?

options.text: So that we can comfort others with the comfort we ourselves received.
options.note: Comfort is given to be passed on to others in any trouble.

options.text: So that we will feel better and move on quickly.
options.note: God does bring relief, but the stated purpose is to equip us to comfort others.

options.text: So that we can prove we are stronger than our trials.
options.note: The point is reliance on God, not a display of our own strength.

options.text: So that we will never face trouble again.
options.note: Paul assumes ongoing affliction, met by ongoing comfort.

quiz.q: What did the deadly peril in Asia teach Paul?

options.text: Not to trust in himself, but in God who raises the dead.
options.note: Verse 9 states this as the very purpose of the ordeal.

options.text: To avoid dangerous places in the future.
options.note: The lesson was about trust in God, not merely caution.

options.text: That his own endurance was the key to survival.
options.note: The lesson was the opposite: to stop relying on himself.

options.text: That God had abandoned him in Asia.
options.note: God delivered him and would deliver again; He had not abandoned him.

quiz.q: What part did the Corinthians play in Paul's deliverance?

options.text: They helped him by their prayers, so that many gave thanks to God.
options.note: Verse 11 shows prayer as a real means God uses, leading to thanksgiving.

options.text: They sent money to fund his escape.
options.note: The text names prayer, not financial aid, as their help here.

options.text: They traveled to Asia to rescue him personally.
options.note: Their help was intercession from a distance, not a rescue mission.

options.text: They did nothing; deliverance was Paul's work alone.
options.note: Paul credits their prayers as part of God's deliverance.

questions: Paul blesses God as the Father of all comfort out of the depths of affliction. When you are pressed by trouble, do you turn first to worship of God, or to something else?
God comforts us so that we can comfort others. Who in your life is in trouble right now, and how might the comfort you have received from God flow through you to them?
The peril in Asia taught Paul to stop trusting himself and to trust the God who raises the dead. Where are you still relying on your own strength instead of on God?

Paul was honest with the Corinthians about despairing of life. Are you willing to be honest with fellow believers about your own struggles, so that they can pray and share the comfort of Christ with you?
The Corinthians helped Paul by their prayers. Whose deliverance might God be inviting you to share in through faithful intercession this week?

translationNotes.note: The words comfort and comforts translate the paraklesis word group, which appears ten times in these five verses; English cannot always show how insistently the theme is repeated in the Greek.
The province of Asia refers to the Roman province in what is now western Turkey, centered on Ephesus, not the continent of Asia.
The sentence of death renders a legal image of a death verdict; Paul felt as good as condemned, so that his hope had to rest entirely on the God who raises the dead.
